name: Sukuma
description: The Sukuma are Tanzania's largest ethnic group, inhabiting the south-eastern shores of Lake Victoria in Shinyanga and Mwanza regions. They are primarily agro-pastoralists, growing cotton and keeping cattle. The Sukuma Federation — a loose confederation of chiefdoms — maintained considerable autonomy through the colonial period. They are famous for the Bugobogobo and Bugobela competitive dance societies, whose performances rival professional entertainment in sophistication and spectacle.
cultural_highlights: Bugobogobo & Bugobela dance societies — two rival competitive dance societies whose annual festivals involve elaborate costumes, acrobatics, and snake-handling performances
Sungusungu vigilante system — community self-defence groups that emerged in the 1980s, rooted in ancient Sukuma communal protection traditions
Chief-and-council system — each chiefdom governed by a ntemi (chief) advised by a council; many chiefdoms unified under the Sukuma Federation
Agricultural calendar rituals — planting and harvest ceremonies tied to rain-calling and ancestral propitiation

food_traditions.name: Ugali (stiff maize porridge)
food_traditions.description: Maize ugali served with fish from Lake Victoria or vegetable stew — the Sukuma daily staple

food_traditions.name: Dagaa (dried sardines)
food_traditions.description: Small dried lake sardines (omena) cooked into stews or eaten dry — a protein staple from Lake Victoria

food_traditions.name: Mtori (banana beef stew)
food_traditions.description: A coastal-influenced stew of unripe banana and beef — popular in Sukuma villages near the lake coast

music_traditions: Bugobogobo dance music — fast-paced drum and horn ensemble driving competitive dance performances with acrobatic elements
Isanzu harp — a traditional harp-lute found among the Sukuma's eastern neighbours, sometimes adopted in ceremonial contexts
Rain-calling songs (imba) — sung by women's groups during droughts to petition the ancestors for rain
